name: Shedim
description: Shedim are demonic figures appearing in Jewish folklore and rabbinic literature. They are often described as spirits or lesser deities, inhabiting desolate places, ruins, and cemeteries. The nature of Shedim is ambiguous; they are neither wholly good nor entirely evil, possessing characteristics of both humans and angels. They are believed to be capable of both helping and harming humans, often acting as tricksters or malevolent entities. Their origins are debated, with some traditions suggesting they are the offspring of Lilith and Adam, while others claim they are the souls of the dead. Shedim are said to have wings, the ability to see the future, and are often associated with illness and misfortune. Protection against Shedim is sought through amulets, incantations, and observance of religious laws. Their existence highlights the complex spiritual landscape of Jewish tradition, encompassing both divine and demonic forces that interact with the human world. Rabbinic texts provide various methods for controlling or warding off these spirits, emphasizing the importance of divine protection and righteous behavior.
subtitle: Demonic spirits in Jewish folklore.
appearance: Shedim are often described as having a human-like appearance, but with wings and sometimes cloven hooves. They can also shapeshift into various animal forms.
habitat: Desolate places, ruins, cemeteries, and uninhabited areas.
behavior: Shedim are known for their mischievous and often malevolent behavior. They can be tricksters, deceivers, and causes of misfortune. However, some Shedim are also said to be helpful, offering knowledge or assistance to humans.

keyMyths.title: The Queen of Sheba and the Shedim
keyMyths.description: In some legends, the Queen of Sheba is said to have had relationships with Shedim, which contributed to her wisdom and power. This tale highlights the ambiguous nature of Shedim, as they can be sources of both knowledge and danger. The Talmud mentions the Queen of Sheba as testing King Solomon with riddles, and some accounts suggest that she was aided by Shedim in this endeavor. This story emphasizes the connection between Shedim and esoteric knowledge.

keyMyths.title: Shedim as Builders of Solomon's Temple
keyMyths.description: According to some traditions, King Solomon used Shedim to help build the First Temple in Jerusalem. He allegedly controlled them through the use of a magical ring, forcing them to perform various tasks. This showcases the belief that Shedim could be controlled by those with sufficient power or knowledge. This narrative underscores the power dynamics between humans and the spirit world, with Solomon acting as a mediator.

keyMyths.title: Protection from Shedim through Mezuzah
keyMyths.description: The Mezuzah, a parchment scroll containing biblical verses affixed to the doorposts of Jewish homes, is believed to offer protection against Shedim and other evil spirits. The verses remind those entering and leaving the house of God's presence and protection. This belief highlights the importance of religious observance in warding off demonic influences and maintaining spiritual safety.

sources.author: Trachtenberg, Joshua
sources.work: Jewish Magic and Superstition: A Study in Folk Religion
sources.description: A comprehensive study of Jewish folklore and beliefs, including detailed information on Shedim and their role in Jewish magic.
sources.text: A comprehensive study of Jewish folklore and beliefs, including detailed information on Shedim and their role in Jewish magic.
